card.intuition: Thread a temporary link from each node's in-order predecessor back to the node itself — this lets you navigate the tree without a stack or recursion, using the tree's own null right pointers as return paths.
card.analogy: Leaving breadcrumbs on a hiking trail: before descending a side path, tie a string back to your current position. Follow the side path normally; when you find your own string, you know you've returned and can cut it.
card.time_complexity: O(n) — each node is visited at most twice (once to set thread, once to use and remove it)
card.space_complexity: O(1) — no call stack, no explicit stack data structure
when_to_use: In-order traversal of a BST in O(1) space (e.g., in memory-constrained embedded systems)
Finding in-order successor without a parent pointer or stack
Interview question: traverse a binary tree without extra space
gotchas: Always remove the threaded link after using it — leaving it creates a cycle in the tree
Finding the in-order predecessor: go left once, then right as far as possible (stopping when right is null OR right is already the current node — the threading check)
Morris traversal modifies the tree temporarily — don't use it if the tree must remain immutable

exercise.prompt: Complete Morris in-order traversal. Thread, traverse, and unthread in O(1) space.
hints: When no thread exists yet, set pre.right = curr then descend: curr = curr.left
When thread exists (pre.right is curr), we've returned from the left subtree — remove thread (pre.right = None), record curr, move right
exercise.test_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self,v,l=None,r=None): self.val=v;self.left=l;self.right=r
root=TreeNode(4,TreeNode(2,TreeNode(1),TreeNode(3)),TreeNode(6,TreeNode(5),TreeNode(7)))
assert morris_inorder(root) == [1,2,3,4,5,6,7], 'BST in-order'
assert morris_inorder(TreeNode(1)) == [1], 'Single'
assert morris_inorder(None) == [], 'Empty'
# Verify tree not permanently modified
assert root.left.left.right is None, 'Thread must be removed'
print('PASS')
